text: On to the show.

text: Welcome to Fragmented, a podcast where we capture what's top of mind for Android developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm your host, Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Today, I want to talk about programming paradigms and specifically about one paradigm called data-oriented programming or DOP.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I came across DOP recently.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it really invigorated my passion as a software developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I haven't been this excited about an approach since dabbling with Rx and functional programming in the recent past.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But before we go any further, what is a programming paradigm?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Object-oriented programming, or oops, as we have come to love and learn, that's a programming paradigm.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Functional programming, that's another programming paradigm.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The goal of any programming paradigm is essentially to manage complexity.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But complexity comes in many forms, and not all paradigms handle all forms of complexity equally well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you look at object-oriented programming, for example...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The key concept is that everything can be modeled as an object.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It excels at modeling real world entities with different complex behaviors and relationships all within your object.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can combine state, behavior, everything and package it in a nice object and just be on your way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Object-oriented programming basically encourages you to do this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You are successful with this paradigm when you define and defend those boundaries in your program.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They have concepts like encapsulation and all of the other things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Functional programming, on the other hand, excels when dealing with data manipulation and calculations where things like immutability matter, like, you know, pure functions are crucial, especially in situations where you have a lot of parallel processing and concurrency going on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It provides a reliable way in these scenarios or situations of just sending inputs in and deterministically getting something out.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The keyword here is functions, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Because I mean, functional programming.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what about data-oriented programming or DOP?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The good news is, by the way, before I even start any of this, DOP can coexist with all of these other paradigms that we have come to love and use.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This isn't a replacement or like a new hammer for us to like just attack all things with or see everything as a nail.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Data-oriented programming is...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: a paradigm concept that you can judiciously use in addition with all your other programs.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's just a tool that forces you to think more critically about how you represent your data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if object-oriented programming is all about objects, everything is an object, and functional programming is all about functions, everything can be modeled as a function,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: DOP is about, well, you guessed it, data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's about making your data representation as the top priority.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the key to understanding DOP is basically understanding the weight that they put in behind that term data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's not the thing that you take casually.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're very, very particular about what you call data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You know, the representation of your data is
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: becomes the most important thing in this paradigm.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: On first reading, by the way, it can be a little vague, but the more and more I read about it, it started to click.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'll point you to all the resources that I used over the course of like trying to come up with this episode because...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I assure you, I don't think I can do full justice in just this short time that we have together and through the audio medium.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But I'll try to at least leave you a little more curious about the topic at the end.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But before I go any further on extolling the virtues of data, I almost feel the better way to make this click immediately is to take a simple example and...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: illustrate how one might think about data or model data in like the world of data oriented programming.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's take the simplest example.

text: Say you have a simple bank transaction class.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Every bank transaction, it's fair to say, requires an ID, right, or an identifier.

text: How would you model this?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's how they become an object.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But data-oriented programming, DOP,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Basically says your data needs to be much more representative.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All those things that we talked about really should be represented directly in the data.

speaker: SPEAKER_00
